subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a high school diploma as highest formal education credential
Completed several online courses on web design and digital graphics (e.g., Udemy, Coursera)
Self-taught in web design principles using free online resources and video tutorials
No formal college or university degree
Attended a local workshop on accessibility in web design

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Graphic Designer (Web Design focus)
Designs and edits webpages for clients using no-code and low-code platforms
Responsible for adapting client ideas into digital layouts and color schemes
Frequently reconstructs existing designs to better suit client needs
Balances visual creativity with usability and accessibility requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the digital design and web development sector
Part of the creative/technology industry
Focuses on small business and personal brand website projects
Operates within the broader technology-enabled services sector

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works independently as a freelancer or in a small design studio (2-5 people)
Remote work is common, with occasional in-person meetings
Collaborates online with clients and occasional project partners
Company culture values flexibility, creativity, and fast turnaround

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Primarily serves small businesses, entrepreneurs, and personal brands
Focuses on web design for non-technical clients needing accessible websites
Works on projects in e-commerce, portfolio sites, and informational business pages
Occasionally assists with rebranding or visual refresh projects for existing websites

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a graphic/web designer
Started working in this role at age 22 or 23
Experience includes both design and editing of web pages
Gained practical experience through freelance projects and client work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considers self an early-career/junior graphic designer
Occupies an entry-level to associate position in the industry
Still building a professional portfolio and expanding client base
Seeks mentorship and peer feedback to accelerate skill growth

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Designs webpage layouts using platforms like Squarespace and Wix
Selects and customizes color schemes for optimal user experience and accessibility
Edits and reconstructs existing webpages to better fit client requirements
Communicates with clients to gather design feedback and requirements
Prepares visual assets using Canva and other design tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 40% of time designing webpage layouts
Dedicates roughly 30% of time to editing and reconstructing existing designs
Allocates 20% of time to color scheme selection and accessibility adjustments
The remaining 10% is spent on client communication and administrative tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Exercises significant creative autonomy within client guidelines
Responsible for major design decisions on most projects
Consults clients for final approval but independently decides most visual elements
Occasionally seeks input from peers or online design communities for tricky problems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Selecting and combining color palettes using built-in tools in Canva and Squarespace
Applying and tweaking pre-made templates to match client branding
Basic webpage layout structuring (headers, footers, navigation)
Resizing and placing images or visual elements within a template

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Customizing templates beyond standard options to achieve unique client requests
Ensuring full accessibility compliance (e.g., color contrast, keyboard navigation)
Troubleshooting layout issues that appear differently on mobile vs. desktop
Integrating third-party widgets or advanced functionality into no-code platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Batch updating color schemes across multiple pages for brand consistency
Reformatting or resizing images for different device breakpoints
Populating template fields with client data or text content
Checking for broken links and basic SEO settings on each webpage

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys experimenting with new color combinations and creative layouts
Finds reconstructing and improving existing designs engaging
Editing text or swapping out images repeatedly can feel tedious
Routine accessibility checks and link testing are necessary but less exciting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Limited customization options in template-based platforms can be frustrating
Manually updating repetitive content across multiple pages is time-consuming
Ensuring accessibility compliance sometimes requires extra research and trial-and-error
Platform integration issues (e.g., importing assets from Canva to Wix) can slow workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive and creative when working on visually engaging layouts
Skill growth is strongest when tackling slightly challenging design problems
Boredom with repetitive tasks can lower motivation and output quality
Enjoyment increases when learning new tricks or shortcuts in design tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Feels enthusiastic about trying out new design tools and features
Regularly explores updates in Squarespace, Wix, and Canva
Views tech learning as essential for staying competitive in the field
Open to recommendations from peers and online design communities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self quick to adapt to new web design platforms
Can learn basic functionality of a new tool within a few days
Occasionally needs extra support for advanced features or integrations
Prefers hands-on experimentation to formal training courses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to spend several hours weekly on self-paced learning
Prioritizes practical skills that can be applied to current projects
Prefers short, focused tutorials over lengthy certifications
Will invest more time if a tool promises significant workflow improvements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the opportunity to speed up processes and impress clients
Main barrier is high workload, which can limit time for self-education
Sometimes hesitates to learn tools that seem only marginally relevant
Cost of paid courses can occasionally be a deterrent

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share recommendations for new design plugins or AI tools
Online design communities are a primary source of tech discovery
As an independent worker, rarely receives direct management pressure
Peer success stories with new tools encourage experimentation

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Squarespace for most client website builds and edits
Relies on Wix for clients who need more flexible design options
Canva is used daily for creating and editing graphics and assets
Occasionally uses browser-based color palette generators (e.g., Coolors)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Browser-based HTML/CSS preview tools for layout checks
Google Drive for storing and sharing design assets with clients
Slack or email for client and team communication
Built-in accessibility checkers in Squarespace or Wix

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with no-code website builders (Squarespace, Wix)
Basic familiarity with image formats (PNG, JPEG, SVG)
Limited experience with Adobe Creative Suite due to cost and learning curve
Knows key principles of responsive design and accessibility

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces formatting issues when importing Canva graphics into Wix
Squarespace and Wix have limited direct integration with third-party design tools
Manually exporting and uploading design assets can be repetitive
Inconsistent rendering of fonts and colors between platforms requires extra checks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts like machine learning, large language models, and generative AI
Knows how to use prompt-based AI tools for text and image generation
Follows AI developments relevant to design through online communities
Recognizes the potential of AI for automating repetitive design tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT for brainstorming design ideas and troubleshooting issues
Has experimented with Canva's Magic Design (AI-powered layout suggestions)
Tried DALL·E for generating unique design assets when needed
Occasionally uses Grammarly (AI writing assistant) for editing website copy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs like ChatGPT regularly, at least several times per week
Applies AI tools mainly for creative brainstorming and generating design variations
Occasionally uses AI for automating routine content generation (e.g., placeholder text)
Relies on AI to help solve tricky layout or color matching challenges

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Used ChatGPT to quickly generate multiple tagline options for a client, reducing brainstorming time
AI-generated color palettes sometimes lack accessibility contrast, requiring manual adjustments
Tried using AI to create a website layout, but the output needed significant editing to meet client standards
Learned that AI is most useful for idea generation, not for final design output

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal in-house training, relies on self-directed learning
Peers in online forums often share AI tool tips and best practices
Finds tutorials and guides on YouTube and design blogs helpful
Occasionally attends free online webinars on AI in graphic design

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers a hybrid approach: starts independently, then consults AI for specific challenges
Uses AI as a support tool rather than a constant collaborator
Finds step-by-step suggestions from AI useful when exploring new design techniques
Appreciates being able to ask AI for help at any point in the process

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for brainstorming and error-checking
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor for accessibility and usability ideas
Does not rely on AI as a co-worker for full project execution
Uses AI to double-check decisions but maintains creative control

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating more AI-powered features if they improve speed and quality
Willing to try new AI tools that automate repetitive design tasks
Cautious about over-relying on AI for creative decisions
Interested in experimenting with AI-driven accessibility checkers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational AI interfaces (e.g., ChatGPT) for brainstorming and troubleshooting
Finds command-based interfaces helpful for batch tasks (e.g., image resizing)
Would like the option to switch between conversational and direct command modes
Values clear, concise AI responses without excessive jargon

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Believes web design requires a mix of technical skills and creative intuition
Many client requests involve subjective judgment not easily captured by AI
Accessibility and brand alignment often require context-specific knowledge
Uses AI mainly for generic suggestions, not for nuanced brand decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for initial drafts and brainstorming but verifies all outputs manually
Rarely accepts AI-generated layouts or color schemes without review
More confident in AI-generated text than design recommendations
Trust level increases as AI tools improve in context awareness

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive or data-driven tasks (e.g., batch resizing, color checks)
Retains human control over creative and client-facing decisions
Sees AI as a partner in efficiency, not a replacement for design sense
Wants the option to override or modify all AI-generated outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for back-end or repetitive design updates
Feels automation is threatening if it replaces client-facing creativity
Wants clear manual override options to retain control
More comfortable with automation when tasks are well-defined and low-risk

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances need for creative and critical thinking
Some technical skills (e.g., basic layout, color picking) may become less critical
Client communication and brand alignment remain highly valuable skills
Views AI as a tool to boost, not replace, core competencies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning how to prompt and evaluate AI-generated designs is a new required skill
Staying current with AI tool updates is becoming part of the job
Responsible for verifying AI outputs for accessibility and client standards
Must explain to clients how AI is used in the design process

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Some concern about AI automating basic design tasks
Believes creative, client-specific work is less at risk of automation
Watches industry trends to stay ahead of potential job changes
Feels moderate job security due to focus on tailored, creative services

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manually reviews all AI-generated outputs before client delivery
Built-in platform tools allow for easy editing of AI-suggested elements
Prefers AI tools that offer transparent, editable suggestions
No processes are fully automated without human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: As an independent worker, sets own policies on AI use
Peers are increasingly open to AI tools for design support
No formal policies, but client transparency about AI usage is growing
Industry shift toward accepting AI as a standard part of the workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a major driver of efficiency and innovation in design
Believes AI will democratize access to professional-quality websites
Worries about potential for generic, cookie-cutter design if overused
Anticipates AI will free up time for more creative and strategic work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports ethical AI use with transparency to clients
Concerned about data privacy when using cloud-based AI design tools
Believes human review is essential to prevent biased or inappropriate AI outputs
Wants fair credit for original creative work, even if AI-assisted

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Wants AI tools that can check and guarantee full accessibility compliance
Desires smarter AI suggestions that account for brand identity and user demographics
Looks for AI that can automate bulk updates across multiple client pages
Seeks better integration between design platforms and AI-powered asset generators

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine design work to become highly automated
Predicts greater focus on creative direction and client strategy
Foresees need to master 'AI prompting' as a core skill
Believes client expectations for rapid turnaround will increase with AI adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Would encourage regular training and knowledge-sharing on new AI tools
Supports setting clear guidelines on when and how to disclose AI use to clients
Recommends investing in accessible, user-friendly AI solutions
Wants ongoing evaluation of AI tools for ethical and creative impact
